comments:
Summary and strengths:
The paper describes a well-executed engineering effort to build an Egyptian Arabic-to-English phrase-based SMT system for the NIST OpenMT’2015 evaluation. The authors address realistic challenges of informal, dialectal corpora (SMS, chat, conversational speech), and present a range of sensible preprocessing and modeling choices: Arabizi-to-Arabic conversion, morphological segmentation (ATB, S2, D3), Egyptian-to-MSA conversion, hierarchically-interpolated LMs, sparse features, class-based models, NNJM, transliteration attempts, phrase-table merging and multi-engine output combination. The experimental section reports controlled ablation-style comparisons showing which components contributed to BLEU improvements, and the system achieved strong leaderboard placement (second across genres). The paper is practically useful to researchers and practitioners working on low-resource/dialectal Arabic MT and multilingual preprocessing.

Weaknesses and concerns:
- The document sample provided is missing the actual tables and many numeric details (likely due to OCR), which limits the ability to fully judge the rigor and magnitude of reported gains. In several places the discussion refers to gains (e.g., up to 3 BLEU for segmentation, +1 BLEU for interpolated LM, smaller gains for NNJM) but the underlying numbers, standard deviations, and statistical significance testing are not visible in the excerpt.
- There is little qualitative error analysis or human evaluation to complement BLEU scores. For informal genres, BLEU can be unreliable; analysis of typical errors (e.g., mistranslated dialect items, named entity handling, segmentation mistakes) would strengthen the paper.
- Some components (e.g., Arabizi conversion, Egyptian->MSA converter, transliteration model) are only briefly described; more details on their accuracy and failure modes, and how conversion errors affect downstream MT, would be valuable.
- Originality is limited: the work mostly integrates and adapts known tools and methods to this specific task. This makes the paper more of an applied systems contribution than a methodological advance, which is fine but should be positioned accordingly.

Recommendations for improvement:
- Include all tables and full numeric results, with confidence intervals or significance testing where appropriate.
- Add a concise error analysis (examples of common failure cases), and if possible some human evaluation (fluency/adequacy) or targeted metrics for dialect phenomena.
- Report sizes of training/development/test data and more implementation details (hyperparameters of NNJM, class counts for clustering, Arabizi conversion accuracy) so others can reproduce and build on the work.
- Discuss runtime and resource requirements and consider releasing preprocessing code or models (Arabizi converter, segmentation scripts) to increase impact.

Overall assessment:
This is a solid applied contribution documenting a competitive system for an important real-world MT challenge. While the novelty is modest, the paper provides useful empirical findings and practical recipes for handling dialectal and noisy Arabic, which should interest the MT community. With fuller presentation of results and some qualitative analysis, this would be a strong systems paper.
